_citation.primary_source: Mahadevan papers (1970-2018), RMRL Digital Library scrape (Phase-31)
_citation.full_reference: Multiple papers by Iravatham Mahadevan, accessed via RMRL Digital Library (https://rmrl.in/en/dl/research-papers/mahadevan). Specific paper citations per entry below.
_citation.license: Each entry's reading is Mahadevan's published proposal in the cited paper. Compilation here is for reference + computational analysis only. Original papers are © respective journals/publishers; archival copies © RMRL.
_citation.see_also: CITATIONS.md section C.5; backend/glossa_lab/data/mahadevan_papers_extracted.json for the supporting evidence base.
_citation.compiled_by: Glossa-Lab Phase-32 T2 — high-confidence direct readings only (Mahadevan's own published claims).
_citation.methodology_note: This is an ADDITIVE map: it should be merged with parpola_phonemes.json, not used in isolation. Each entry is Mahadevan's own proposal from his published papers as scraped via the RMRL flipbook viewer's full-text OCR (book_config.js textForPages array). Only entries where Mahadevan explicitly states a Dravidian rebus reading are included.

_doc: Augmented phoneme map derived from Mahadevan's research papers (Phase-32 paper-text mining). 11 high-confidence new/strengthened entries beyond the Parpola map. Each entry cites the specific paper + page where Mahadevan proposes the reading.
_methodology_note: Sign IDs use Parpola's numbering (consistent with parpola_phonemes.json). DEDR refs are from Burrow & Emeneau 1984 (Dravidian Etymological Dictionary, 2nd ed.). Mahadevan often uses a non-standard romanization (e.g. 'pa$l\i' for 'pali'); I have transcribed to standard scholarly romanization here.

48.source: Mahadevan 1989 'What do we know about the Indus script', Mahadevan 2007 'A Megalithic pottery inscription'
48.homonymy: Mahadevan identifies sign 48 as a generic 'deity' marker (cf. Sumerian {d} divine determinative). Frequency on votive tablets and sealings is markedly higher than seal context, supporting the determinative/divinity reading.
48.note: 20 mentions across 8 papers; one of Mahadevan's most-defended readings (1973-2018).

akam_unspecified.source: Mahadevan 'Akam and Puram - Address signs of Indus script' (~2010s)
akam_unspecified.iconic: (specific Indus sign id not yet integer-resolved)
akam_unspecified.homonymy: Old Tamil 'akam' = inside / inner city / heart (DEDR 7); used in classical Tamil grammar (Tolkappiyam Porul.20:12) for 'inner-fortified-citadel' as opposed to 'puram' (outer).

puram_unspecified.source: Mahadevan 'Akam and Puram - Address signs of Indus script' (~2010s)
puram_unspecified.homonymy: Old Tamil 'puram' = outside / outer city (DEDR 4333); 'pirai' = crescent moon (DEDR 4422). Mahadevan reads the crescent-moon sign as the 'outer' marker via rebus on pirai/puram cognates.

palli_unspecified.gloss: planned city / city of four quarters
palli_unspecified.source: Mahadevan 'Akam and Puram - Address signs of Indus script' (~2010s)
palli_unspecified.homonymy: Old Tamil 'palli' = village/township (DEDR 4112); cognate forms include 'palli' = settlement (DEDR 4113).

_muruku_47_strengthened.gloss: Murukan / war-god / young man (also: ear-ring / bangle)
_muruku_47_strengthened.source: Mahadevan 1999 'Murukan in the Indus script'; Mahadevan 2006 'A note on the Muruku sign'; Mahadevan 2008 'Meluhha and Agastya'
_muruku_47_strengthened.homonymy: Mahadevan defends the 'muruku' reading specifically for sign 47 in the Murukan paper, identifying compounds with the war-god name. 29 mentions of sign 47 across his Murukan-themed papers (1999-2008).
_muruku_47_strengthened.note: STRENGTHENS Parpola's reading of sign 261 (intersecting circles) = muruku. Mahadevan extends muruku to sign 47 directly as well via fish-family rebus.

_great_god_blue_neck.gloss: blue-necked one (Siva); the Indus 'great god' as bilingual clue to Dravidian myth
_great_god_blue_neck.source: Mahadevan 2007 'How did the great god get a blue neck — a bilingual clue to the Indus script'
_great_god_blue_neck.iconic: (specific sign assignment requires the paper's figs; not yet integer-resolved)
_great_god_blue_neck.homonymy: The 'blue-necked' (Sanskrit nilakantha) name of Siva is a calque of Dravidian 'kar-mani-galam' (dark-jewel-throat). Mahadevan argues the Indus name for the proto-Siva figure was a Dravidian compound that was later translated into Sanskrit when Siva was assimilated into the Vedic pantheon.

_meluhha_agastya.source: Mahadevan 2008 'Meluhha and Agastya - Alpha and Omega of the Indus script'; Mahadevan 1986 'Agastya legend and the Indus civilization'
_meluhha_agastya.homonymy: Mahadevan argues 'Meluhha' (Akkadian endo/exonym for Indus speakers) and 'Agastya' (the Vedic seer who 'crossed the Vindhyas to the south') are bilingual end-points of an Indus → Dravidian → Sanskrit lineage.

_agricultural_terms.gloss: tenant / labourer / yield / rent (5 DEDR-cross-refs in Mahadevan 2006)
_agricultural_terms.source: Mahadevan 2006 'Agricultural terms in the Indus script'
_agricultural_terms.homonymy: Mahadevan proposes a constellation of agricultural-economic readings: 'tenant' (DEDR 1655), 'labourer' (DEDR 399), 'rent' (DEDR 5359), 'yield' (DEDR 5437). Specific sign assignments require the paper figures (not yet integer-resolved).
_agricultural_terms.note: 18 sign references in this paper; rich content for future fine-grained extraction.

_137_unspecified.phoneme: (uncertain — Mahadevan's papers have 7 mentions but no single defended reading)
_137_unspecified.gloss: frequent sign in Mahadevan's Mohenjodaro context analyses
_137_unspecified.source: Mahadevan 2007 'A Megalithic pottery inscription'; Mahadevan 1987 'Archaeological context of Indus texts at Mohenjodaro'
_137_unspecified.note: 7 mentions; placeholder for future fine extraction.

_176_unspecified.gloss: frequent sign in unicorn-seal cult-object discussions
_176_unspecified.source: Mahadevan 1983 'The Cult objects on unicorn seals'

_328_342_pottery.gloss: signs that recur in megalithic-pottery inscription contexts
_328_342_pottery.source: Mahadevan 2001 'Indus like symbols on megalithic pottery'; Mahadevan 2007 'A Megalithic pottery inscription'
_328_342_pottery.note: Mahadevan repeatedly cites these signs as appearing on Tamil-Nadu megalithic pottery — KEY evidence for Indus-Tamil cultural continuity if confirmed.

_evidence_base_pointer: backend/glossa_lab/data/mahadevan_papers_extracted.json contains the full extracted contexts (200-char windows) for all 100 sign-references and 26 DEDR-pairings across the 53 RMRL papers. Use that file for downstream phoneme-expansion work.
